mainmatter.p: Let { \left ( V,I, \otimes , \alpha , \lambda , \rho \right )} be a monoidal category. A category C enriched in V is defined by the following structure and axioms:

ol.li: for any two objects x,y, an object \mathbf {hom} _{ C } { \left ( x , y \right )} \in V;
for every object x, a morphism I \xrightarrow {i _{ x } } \mathbf {hom} _{ C } { \left ( x , x \right )} in V;

for all objects x,y,z, a morphism \mathbf {hom} _{ C } { \left ( x , y \right )} \otimes \mathbf {hom} _{ C } { \left ( y , z \right )} \xrightarrow {c _{ x,y,z } } \mathbf {hom} _{ C } { \left ( x , z \right )} in V.
such that the following diagrams commute in V:

mainmatter.p: A monoidal category is a category C equipped with the following structure and axioms:

ol.li: a functor C \times C \xrightarrow { \otimes }C called the tensor product;
an object I \in C called the tensor unit;
a natural isomorphism { \left ( x \otimes y \right )} \otimes z \xrightarrow { \alpha _{ x,y,z } } x \otimes { \left ( y \otimes z \right )} called the associator;
a natural isomorphism I \otimes x \xrightarrow { \lambda _{ x } }x called the left unitor;
a natural isomorphism x \otimes I \xrightarrow { \rho _{ x } }x called the right unitor;
such that the triangle below commutes:

mainmatter.p: There is a common thread in modern approaches to domain theory, theory of computation, and topology: one has an object \Sigma with two points \bot , \top : \Sigma that behave like the Sierpiński space, and it induces a binary “order” relation on any other object by serving as an interval: in particular, for a space X and points x,y:X we may say that x \sqsubseteq y when there exists a mapping H \colon \Sigma \to X such that H \bot = x and H \top = y. This is called the path order.

In addition to the path order described above, the Sierpiński space has its own intrinsic order: for p,q: \Sigma we say that p \leq q when p= \top implies q= \top. The Phoa principle is an important law governing the Sierpiński space ensuring that the path order and the intrinsic order coincide.

The map \Sigma ^ \Sigma \to \Sigma \times \Sigma sending f to the pair { \left ( f \bot ,f \top \right )} is an embedding and its image is the subset of \Sigma \times \Sigma spanned by pairs { \left ( p,q \right )} such that p= \top implies q= \top.

A concrete model of domain theory (like cpos) is always a poset-enriched category, which can also be thought of as a (locally posetal) 2-category. I have discovered that it is possible to re-formulate Phoa’s principle in terms of 2-categorical universal properties of comma and cocomma squares, and it can be shown that in a cartesian closed locally posetal 2-category, an appropriate version of Phoa’s principle always holds.

In fact,
an appropriate version of Phoa’s principle should hold in any cartesian closed 2-category (including non-posetal ones), and the proposal is to prove that this is the case using string diagrams
. This result will have implications for the understanding of higher-dimensional domain theory as applied to concurrency.

A category with zero morphisms is defined to be a category enriched in the category of pointed sets and strict maps. The distinguished point of \mathbf {hom} _{ \mathcal {A} } { \left ( A , B \right )} shall be written 0.
